text: As of 2024, medical assistance in dying (MAID) is legal in 10 U.S. states plus Washington D.C., Canada, the Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg, Spain, Colombia, and several other countries. Canada's MAID program has expanded dramatically — from 1,018 deaths in 2016 to over 13,000 in 2022, accounting for 4.1% of all Canadian deaths. Supporters call it the ultimate expression of personal autonomy. Critics, including many disability rights advocates, argue that when the healthcare system fails people, offering death as an option isn't freedom — it's abandonment. Where do you stand?

options.text: Fundamental right — competent adults with terminal illness should have the legal right to choose when and how they die; denying this is imposing suffering in the name of principle
Dangerous path — Canada's expansion shows how quickly eligibility broadens; what starts as terminal illness becomes chronic pain, then mental illness, then disability; the slope is real
Right in principle, dangerous in practice — assisted dying is ethical when palliative care, mental health support, and disability services are fully funded; without those, it's a false choice
The autonomy framing is incomplete — no one makes this choice in a vacuum; when society signals that some lives aren't worth living through inadequate support, "choosing" death isn't really choosing
None of these / I see it differently

B.prompt: If the slope is real, where exactly would you draw the line — and on what principle? Terminal illness only? Unbearable physical suffering? What about someone with treatment-resistant depression who has suffered for 20 years?

text: Americans spend roughly 25% of all Medicare dollars — over $200 billion annually — in the last year of life, with much of it on aggressive interventions that extend life by days or weeks in ICU settings. Atul Gawande's Being Mortal (2014) documented how the medical system's default is to treat aggressively even when patients would prefer comfort care. A 2023 Kaiser Family Foundation study found that 72% of Americans want to die at home, but only 31% do. What's driving the disconnect?

options.text: The medical system's incentives — fee-for-service rewards intervention over conversation; doctors are trained to treat, not to help people die well; the system is structurally incapable of stopping
Cultural denial of death — Americans refuse to plan for dying; families demand "do everything" because they haven't had the conversation; the system responds to what people ask for
Both — the medical system profits from aggressive treatment AND families avoid the conversation; neither side has an incentive to stop, and the dying patient is caught in between
Inequality — wealthy, educated patients get palliative care and die comfortably at home; poor patients get the ICU; the "disconnect" is really a class divide in death, like everything else
None of these / I see it differently

text: Hospice care — comfort-focused treatment for patients expected to live six months or less — has been shown to improve quality of life and, paradoxically, often extends survival compared to aggressive treatment. Yet only 47% of Medicare beneficiaries used hospice in 2022, and the median stay was just 9 days despite eligibility for 6 months. Simultaneously, the hospice industry has attracted private equity — by 2023, for-profit hospices outnumbered nonprofits, and investigations revealed patterns of enrolling patients who weren't dying (to collect per diem payments) while skimping on care for those who were. Is hospice working?

options.text: The model works but the incentives are broken — hospice as medical philosophy is sound; hospice as a profit center is corrupt; ban for-profit hospice or regulate it as heavily as nursing homes
Hospice is underutilized because the medical system resists it — doctors refer too late because admitting a patient is dying feels like failure; the problem is medical culture, not hospice itself

The entire end-of-life care system needs redesign — the binary between aggressive treatment and hospice is artificial; what people need is a continuum that shifts gradually from cure-focused to comfort-focused
Private equity in hospice is a symptom of a larger disease — when profit motive enters any caregiving space (nursing homes, mental health, hospice), quality declines and vulnerable people suffer
None of these / I see it differently

text: Cryonics — preserving the body or brain at ultra-low temperatures after legal death in hope of future revival — has attracted roughly 500 people preserved and several thousand signed up as of 2024, mostly through Alcor Life Extension Foundation and the Cryonics Institute. Membership costs range from $28,000 to $200,000. Proponents, including some neuroscientists, argue that information preservation is plausible even if revival isn't currently possible. Mainstream science considers it pseudoscience. Is it a rational bet, an expression of privilege, or something else?

options.text: Rational bet — if there's any nonzero probability of revival and you value your continued existence, the expected value calculation favors preservation; it's just Pascal's wager with liquid nitrogen
Pseudoscience exploiting fear of death — there is no evidence that identity survives the damage of current preservation methods; it's an expensive fantasy for people who can't accept mortality

An expression of techno-privilege — the same worldview that produces billionaire longevity research and Mars colonization; the refusal to accept human limits, available only to those who can afford it

Harmless but revealing — what cryonics tells us isn't about the technology; it's about how uncomfortable modern secular culture is with death and how desperate people are for any alternative to finality
None of these / I see it differently

text: Across cultures, death practices vary enormously: Tibetan sky burials expose the body to vultures; Ghanaian fantasy coffins celebrate the deceased's life and identity; Mexican Día de los Muertos maintains ongoing relationships with the dead; and traditional Irish wakes involve communal drinking and storytelling with the body present. Modern Western death has become increasingly medicalized and sanitized — most Americans have never seen a dead body outside a funeral home. Sociologist Philippe Ariès called this "the invisible death." Has something been lost?

options.text: Yes — removing death from daily life makes it more terrifying, not less; the sanitization of death is why modern cultures handle grief and mortality so poorly
No — medicalization and professional death care are improvements; romanticizing pre-modern death practices ignores that they often reflected poverty and necessity, not wisdom
The loss is real but the solution isn't nostalgia — what's needed is a modern death culture that integrates mortality into life without returning to pre-scientific practices

This is a class and culture question — immigrant communities, religious communities, and rural communities still have rich death practices; "invisible death" is a specific problem of secular, affluent, Western society
None of these / I see it differently

text: Philosopher Shelly Kagan at Yale argues that death is bad only because it deprives us of future good experiences — the "deprivation account." Epicurus argued 2,300 years ago that death is nothing to us because when death exists, we don't, and when we exist, death doesn't. Existentialists like Heidegger claimed that awareness of death is what gives life meaning — "being-toward-death" is what makes authentic living possible. Terror Management Theory, developed by psychologists Sheldon Solomon, Jeff Greenberg, and Tom Pyszczynski, proposes that most of human culture is an elaborate defense mechanism against death anxiety. Which framework comes closest to how you actually experience mortality?

options.text: Deprivation — death is bad because it takes away a future I want; the fear is rational and proportional to what's lost
Epicurean — intellectually, I know death isn't experienced; the anxiety is about imagining absence, which is a cognitive error, not a genuine threat
Existentialist — mortality gives life urgency and meaning; without death, nothing would matter; the awareness is painful but essential
Terror Management — most of what I do is probably death avoidance wearing different masks; I can see it intellectually but can't fully escape it emotionally
None of these / I see it differently

text: If for-profit hospice is the problem, the obvious solution is to restrict hospice to nonprofit organizations. But nonprofits face their own challenges — limited capital, lower wages that create staffing shortages, and geographic gaps in coverage. What's the right model?

options.text: Government-run hospice — Medicare already pays for it; cut out the middleman entirely and make hospice a public service, like the VA for end-of-life care
Nonprofit with public funding — keep hospice independent but fund it like public schools; guaranteed funding removes the incentive to game enrollment
Regulate for-profit heavily — the problem isn't the profit motive itself; it's the absence of quality metrics, enforcement, and penalties; regulate hospice the way we should regulate nursing homes
Shift the model entirely — instead of a separate hospice system, integrate palliative care into every stage of serious illness; the artificial separation between treatment and comfort is the problem
None of these / I see it differently

text: If you could design your own death — timing, setting, who's present, level of awareness — what would it look like, and what does that ideal reveal about what you think a good death actually is?
